Underlying Combined Ratio and Margin Discipline

The underlying combined ratio for the quarter was 92.1%, demonstrating solid underlying profitability despite a challenging environment. Management reaffirmed its full‑year guidance for an underlying combined ratio of 90.5%–91.5% and a GAAP combined ratio of 96.5%–97.5%, assuming 6 points of catastrophe losses.

Reserve Stability Supports Confidence

Management reported no prior‑year casualty reserve development at either the segment or line‑of‑business level in the quarter. This lack of adverse development signals stable reserving trends and underpins confidence in the adequacy of carried reserves amid elevated industry loss severity.

Personal Lines Show Improvement Despite Shrinkage

Personal Lines performance improved meaningfully, with the combined ratio dropping to 92.8% from 98.0% in the prior‑year quarter and from 100.6% for full‑year 2025. While overall personal premiums fell 6%, the company’s target mass affluent personal segment grew new business by 1%, reflecting a deliberate focus on higher‑quality accounts.

E&S Segment Delivers Strong Profitability

The Excess & Surplus segment posted a notably profitable 89.5% combined ratio, an improvement of 3 points versus a year ago. E&S premiums written edged up 1%, supported by average renewal pure price increases of 4.1% as Selective leaned into profitable niches despite competitive pressures.

Renewal Pricing Momentum Offsets Loss Trends

Selective reported robust renewal price increases across key commercial lines, particularly in liability and auto. General liability pricing was up about 9.8%, commercial auto gained roughly 9.1% with auto liability approaching 12%, and excluding workers’ compensation, overall renewal pure price increased 8%.

Investment Income Powers Earnings

After‑tax net investment income climbed 18% year over year to $113 million, providing a significant earnings tailwind. Management said investment income contributed roughly 13.3 points to ROE, while reaffirming that the portfolio’s average credit quality remains A+ with a modestly extended duration of 4.3 years.

Capital Returns and Buyback Flexibility

The company continued returning capital to shareholders, repurchasing $30 million of stock in the quarter after $86 million in buybacks during 2025. With $140 million still authorized and a dividend policy targeting 20%–25% of earnings, Selective signaled ample capacity to balance growth investments with disciplined capital returns.

Technology and AI Drive Productivity

Management emphasized ongoing operational and technology gains, particularly from AI and automation initiatives. An AI claims ingestion system has processed more than half a million documents, and an automated contractual risk transfer tool now delivers over 90% of results within two minutes, all under a formal governance framework with human oversight.

Top‑Line Pressure Weighs on Growth

Despite better margins, top‑line metrics softened, with total premiums down 1% year over year as Standard Commercial Lines slipped 1% and E&S grew only 1%. Standard Personal Lines net premiums fell 6%, largely due to lower new business hit ratios as the company pushed for rate adequacy and tightened underwriting.

Elevated GAAP Combined Ratio and Cats

The reported GAAP combined ratio rose to 98.3%, reflecting both normal first‑quarter seasonality and 6.2 points of catastrophe losses. Management framed the result as consistent with expectations and built its full‑year outlook around typical seasonal patterns, assuming a similar catastrophe load.

Retention and Portfolio Mix Shifts

Overall policy retention held at 82% sequentially but declined 3 points from a year earlier as Selective deliberately shed underperforming business. These underwriting and pricing actions helped protect margins but also contributed to the modest decline in premium volume and a shift toward more profitable cohorts.

Commercial Casualty and Social Inflation Headwinds

Executives flagged elevated commercial casualty loss trends and social inflation as key industry challenges, particularly in general liability and commercial auto. They cited adverse emergence across the sector and external benchmarks showing high loss levels, underscoring the need for continued pricing discipline and tightened terms.

General Liability Under Pressure

General liability performance in the quarter was weaker, with the underlying combined ratio running 2.3 points higher than full‑year 2025 levels. Management said it is building higher severity assumptions into its loss picks and noted that market pricing has not yet fully caught up with these worsening loss trends.

Property Pricing Competition in E&S

Within E&S, property pricing turned slightly negative amid heightened competition, despite still‑strong segment margins. The company acknowledged that this competitive backdrop is pressuring rate levels in certain property markets and is being managed carefully to avoid compromising profitability.

Workers’ Comp Favorability Tapers Off

The quarter did not benefit from the kind of favorable workers’ compensation reserve development seen in prior years, particularly from fourth‑quarter tail reviews. Management stressed a conservative approach to recent accident years, limiting reliance on additional reserve releases from this historically profitable line.

Expense Leverage Risk as Growth Moderates

With premium growth currently muted, Selective highlighted expense management and operating leverage as areas of focus. Technology investments are expanding the company’s capacity, but if top‑line growth stays subdued, there is a risk that expenses could weigh more heavily on margins in the near term.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, Selective reaffirmed its January outlook, including a 2026 GAAP combined ratio of 96.5%–97.5% and an underlying combined ratio of 90.5%–91.5%. The company also guided to after‑tax net investment income of $465 million, an effective tax rate near 21.5%, and maintained its capital‑return framework centered on dividends and opportunistic buybacks.

Selective’s earnings call painted a picture of a disciplined insurer prioritizing sustainable profitability over rapid growth in a difficult market. For investors, the story is one of resilient margins, strong investment income, and tight risk management, set against modest premium contraction and persistent casualty headwinds that will test management’s pricing and underwriting resolve.

Management emphasized this metric as evidence of underlying operating strength and positive operating leverage, with revenue growth outpacing expense increases despite pockets of pressure elsewhere.

Net Interest Margin Expands Year over Year

Net interest margin improved to 3.44%, up 13 basis points from Q1 2025, driven by higher earning-asset yields and a lower cost of funds. The bank squeezed an extra 7 basis points from asset yields while trimming funding costs by 7 basis points, demonstrating disciplined pricing in a still-volatile rate landscape.

Loan and Deposit Franchises Continue to Grow

Average loans rose by about $157 million, or roughly 2%, year over year, with total loans reaching $8.64 billion, up 3.3% versus March 31, 2025. On the funding side, average deposits and customer repurchase agreements increased $288 million, or 2.4%, to $12.5 billion, reinforcing the bank’s core relationship strength.

Robust Loan Origination Activity Despite Rate Pressure

Loan originations in Q1 2026 were about 90% higher than a year earlier and 15% above Q4 2025, signaling healthy demand and effective market coverage. New loan production carried average yields near 6%, though management noted these yields were around 25 basis points lower than last quarter as competition intensified for high-quality credits.

Efficiency Gains and Expense Discipline

Operating efficiency continued to improve, with the efficiency ratio ticking down to 45.8% from 46.3% in Q4 2025 and 46.7% a year ago. Core operating expenses, excluding acquisition-related items and one-time provisions, were essentially flat, highlighting strong cost control while absorbing integration and growth investments.

Credit Metrics Remain Benign Overall

Asset quality stayed solid, with nonperforming loans at just $6.1 million, representing 0.07% of total loans, and classified loans at $83.1 million, still under 1% of the portfolio. The allowance for credit losses stood at $80.2 million, giving the bank a sizable buffer against potential future losses as the cycle matures.

Capital Strength and Book Value Expansion

Shareholders’ equity totaled $2.3 billion, supporting a tangible common equity ratio of 10.5% and a common equity Tier 1 ratio of 16.3%. Tangible book value per share climbed 9% year over year to $11.42, demonstrating that retained earnings and disciplined capital management are translating into tangible value growth for investors.

Heritage Acquisition Extends Bay Area Footprint

Management reported that the Heritage Bank of Commerce acquisition closed and early integration is progressing well, opening the Bay Area as a new growth market. The deal also adds balance sheet flexibility, with plans to sell Heritage’s single-family mortgage pools to optimize capital and reposition earning assets over coming quarters.

Quarterly Earnings and Net Interest Income Dip

On a sequential basis, net earnings slipped from $55.0 million in Q4 2025 to $51.0 million in Q1 2026, with EPS easing from $0.40 to $0.38. Net interest income declined to $117.8 million from $122.7 million, reflecting two fewer calendar days, a $134 million drop in earning assets and the absence of $3.2 million in nonaccrual interest that boosted last quarter.

Loan Yields Face Competitive Compression

Average loan yield fell to 5.32% from 5.47% last quarter as intense rate competition weighed on pricing, particularly for top-tier borrowers. Although new originations still averaged about 6% yields, management stressed that maintaining credit quality is taking precedence over stretching for higher rates in a crowded market.

Seasonal and Utilization Effects on Loan Balances

Total loans fell $56 million from year-end 2025, driven mainly by a seasonal $117 million decline in dairy, livestock and agribusiness line usage as utilization dropped from 78% to 69%. Commercial and industrial line usage also edged down from 32% to 30%, modestly reducing C&I balances despite strong origination volumes.

Uptick in Classified Loans and Specific Reserves

Classified loans increased to $83.1 million from $52.7 million at year-end as the bank tagged more credits for closer monitoring, though the level remains below 1% of total loans. A $2.9 million C&I credit was downgraded to nonperforming and a specific reserve of $3.2 million was recorded, nudging nonperforming loans higher by $1.5 million quarter over quarter.

Net Recoveries Moderate from Prior Quarter

Net recoveries slipped to just $9,000 in Q1 2026 compared with $325,000 in Q4 2025, providing less of a tailwind to provision expense. Management framed this as part of a normalization process from unusually strong recovery trends rather than a sign of broad deterioration in the loan portfolio.

Securities Portfolio Hit by Mark-to-Market Losses

The investment portfolio continued to face rate-driven volatility, with unrealized losses on available-for-sale securities inching up to $310 million from $308 million at year-end. Total investment securities declined $116 million to $4.8 billion as the bank managed duration and liquidity, but the embedded losses remain a swing factor for reported equity.

Fair Value Hedges Add Modest Negative Carry

The $700 million of fair value hedges in place to manage interest-rate risk generated negative carry, reducing interest income by $1.1 million versus Q1 2025 and $750,000 versus Q4 2025. While these hedges dampen earnings in the near term, management views them as an important tool to stabilize balance sheet value against rate volatility.

Noninterest Income and Deposit Mix Pressure

Noninterest income fell to $14.3 million from $16.2 million a year earlier, an 11.7% decline, though results improved relative to Q4 2025, which was hit by a securities loss. Average noninterest-bearing deposits decreased by $112 million year over year and $107 million sequentially, partly offset by growth in interest-bearing nonmaturity balances and repurchase agreements as customers sought yield.

Macro and CRE Risks on the Horizon

Management’s economic outlook, informed by a Moody’s-weighted forecast, calls for sub-1% real GDP growth in the second half of 2026 and unemployment approaching 5% by mid-2026. They also expect commercial real estate prices to keep declining through the end of 2026, a trend that could pressure CRE exposures and demands conservative underwriting.

Forward-Looking Outlook and Strategic Priorities

Guidance remained qualitative, with leaders pointing to Q1 results and balance sheet positioning as the basis for Q2–Q3 actions, including selling Heritage’s mortgage pools by quarter end to free capacity. Capital returns, such as dividends and potential buybacks after last year’s 4.2 million share repurchase, will be reassessed once Heritage integration is further along, while the bank navigates a low-growth, higher-credit-risk economy.

Cvb Financial’s call painted a picture of a bank balancing solid fundamentals with thoughtful caution as the cycle matures and competition intensifies. Investors heard a story of durable profitability, improving efficiency and capital strength, offset by modest earnings pressure, securities marks and a more challenging macro and CRE backdrop, leaving execution on integration and credit discipline as key themes to watch.

Neptune Insurance Holdings, Inc. delivered a confident earnings call that balanced strong growth with a candid view of risks. Management highlighted double‑digit revenue gains, record efficiency, and rapid AI‑driven innovation, while acknowledging seasonal margin pressure, a slight retention dip, and macro headwinds like a sluggish housing market and weather uncertainty.

Record Q1 Revenue and Profitability

Neptune posted Q1 2026 revenue of $37.8 million, up about 28.8% year over year, underscoring robust demand for its specialty insurance offerings. Net income reached $7.3 million and adjusted net income hit $13.4 million, while adjusted EBITDA climbed 26% to $21.6 million, showcasing strong profitability alongside growth.

Strong Margin Profile and Updated Guidance

The company reported a Q1 adjusted EBITDA margin of roughly 57.1%, pressured by seasonal factors and front‑loaded public‑company costs. Despite that, management reiterated a full‑year margin target of 60%–61% and raised full‑year revenue guidance to $195 million, signaling confidence that higher profitability will materialize as the year progresses.

Premium Growth and New Business Momentum

Written premium reached $86.7 million in Q1, fueling around 32% year‑over‑year growth in premium in force. That metric ended the quarter near $389 million, with record first‑quarter new‑business sales indicating that Neptune is still gaining traction in the private flood market despite macro drag.

Operational Efficiency at Record Levels

Trailing 12‑month revenue per employee rose to $2.8 million, with adjusted EBITDA per employee at $1.7 million, both at record highs and up double digits from a year ago. These figures highlight the strength of Neptune’s asset‑light managing general agent model and its ability to scale without heavy headcount growth.

AI and Product Innovation Acceleration

Neptune’s AI push featured prominently, with Atlas+ now in beta as an AI assistant for agents, already generating thousands of interactions and early policy sales. The Neptune app is available inside ChatGPT, and the internal AI tool Proteus handled more than 30% of engineering tickets in March, driving roughly a 50% increase in engineering throughput.

Expanded Capacity Relationships

On the capital side, Neptune renewed one of its eight reinsurance programs with an increased size for the 2026–2027 treaty period. It also added two new reinsurers, expanding its panel to 42 capacity providers and signaling strong long‑term confidence from underwriting partners in Neptune’s risk selection and portfolio performance.

Distribution Scale and Agent Adoption

Agent engagement continues to be a growth engine, with more than 45,000 individual agents signing up for direct platform access since December. About 11,000 of those have already bound new business policies, reflecting strong adoption and providing Neptune with significant leverage as it scales distribution without large marketing spend.

Balance Sheet Actions and Shareholder Returns

The board authorized a $100 million share repurchase plan to be funded by free cash flow over roughly two years, signaling management’s conviction in long‑term value. Neptune also kept de‑leveraging, trimming its revolver to about $222 million and maintaining leverage near 2.2 times trailing adjusted EBITDA, with an intent to stay below roughly 2.5 times.

Q1 Margin Pressures and Seasonality

While margins remained high, the Q1 adjusted EBITDA margin of about 57.1% fell below the full‑year goal due to normal seasonality and front‑loaded audit and compliance expenses. Management framed these pressures as timing issues rather than structural problems, stressing that profitability should normalize at higher levels in later quarters.

Slight Dip in Revenue Retention

Revenue retention slipped modestly to around 90%, down from roughly 92% on a trailing basis in 2025. The company linked this mainly to slower renewal price increases, which have eased from about 13% last year to the mid to high single digits so far this year, a trade‑off between retention and pricing power.

Housing Market Headwind

A sluggish housing market is limiting Neptune’s ability to convert government flood policyholders into private coverage at the pace it would like. Management emphasized that stronger real estate turnover would be a material tailwind for growth, but for now slow transaction volumes are constraining the speed of new‑business acceleration.

Limited Direct-to-Consumer Traction

Neptune’s direct‑to‑consumer business remains a small slice of the pie, historically around 2% of overall volume. While the ChatGPT integration raises brand visibility, it currently cannot bind policies directly, and management flagged that consumer behavior and adoption of this channel remain uncertain.

Product Expansion Constraints

Beyond flood, Neptune’s earthquake product is still in beta and constrained by limited historical event data and ongoing product‑market‑fit testing. Management signaled that any meaningful expansion into new perils will require robust datasets and validation, which could slow diversification but should support disciplined underwriting.

Weather and Policy Uncertainty

Hurricane season remains a major uncontrollable variable, and potential changes in government flood programs add another layer of uncertainty. While Neptune believes it is prepared for different scenarios, shifts in storm activity or policy rules could affect near‑term results and will be closely watched by investors.

Competitive and Market-Structure Risks

The National Flood Insurance Program still controls about 85% of the market, keeping private players in a minority share position. Neptune noted that competition has not yet materially hurt its performance but cautioned that new entrants often struggle after major storms, and regulatory changes could reshape the landscape.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, Neptune raised its full‑year 2026 revenue target to $195 million and reiterated an adjusted EBITDA margin goal of 60%–61%, calling 60% more of a floor than a ceiling. Backed by strong Q1 momentum, record efficiency metrics, a $100 million buyback plan, and ongoing debt reduction, management expressed confidence in sustaining profitable growth despite external headwinds.

Neptune’s earnings call painted a picture of a high‑growth, high‑margin insurer leaning heavily on technology and capital‑light scaling, while staying realistic about cyclical and structural risks. For investors, the key takeaway is a business with expanding fundamentals and aggressive innovation, tempered by the inherent volatility of weather, regulation, and the housing market.

Strong quarterly earnings and AFFO growth

Essential Properties opened the quarter with momentum, posting GAAP net income of $60.0 million and AFFO of $105.8 million. AFFO per share reached $0.50, rising 11% year over year and underscoring solid earnings expansion in what remains a choppy macro backdrop.

Robust capital deployment and equity raises

The REIT stayed firmly on offense, deploying $389 million into 126 properties during the first quarter, signaling confidence in its acquisition pipeline. To support this growth, the company raised about $419 million of equity, including a large overnight deal, and ended the period with $541 million of unsettled forward equity still to be deployed.

Raised full-year guidance

Reflecting this strong start, management lifted 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $2.00 to $2.05, implying around 7% growth at the midpoint. They also stepped up full‑year investment volume expectations by $100 million, now targeting between $1.1 billion and $1.5 billion of acquisitions.

Healthy balance sheet and liquidity

Balance sheet strength remained a central theme, with available liquidity of $1.5 billion providing ample dry powder for future deals. Pro forma net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDAre stood at just 3.5x, while income‑producing gross assets surpassed $7.5 billion, underscoring the scale and financial flexibility of the platform.

High-quality portfolio metrics

The company’s portfolio spanned 2,417 properties leased to more than 400 tenants, with a weighted average remaining lease term of roughly 15 years. Near‑term lease rollover risk appeared limited, with just 2.8% of annual base rent expiring over the next three years and occupancy running at a robust 99.7%.

Positive same-store and credit trends

Same‑store rent grew 1.4% in the quarter, showing steady organic expansion from built‑in escalators. Rent coverage across the portfolio remained strong at 3.5x, and the share of annual base rent under 1.5x coverage fell by 140 basis points, suggesting incremental improvement in tenant credit quality.

Attractive investment yield and underwriting

On new investments, Essential Properties continued to lock in compelling economics, reporting an initial cap rate of 7.7% and a GAAP yield of 8.8%. These deals came with a weighted average initial lease term of 17.7 years, average rent bumps of 2.1%, and unit‑level rent coverage around 3.1x, indicating disciplined underwriting at favorable spreads to its cost of capital.

Notable Denny’s portfolio acquisition

One highlight of the quarter was the acquisition of 74 Denny’s restaurants for $147 million, or under $2 million per property. Management framed the deal as an example of relationship‑driven sourcing and a granular asset profile that should be easier to re‑tenant, supporting both risk management and long‑term cash flow durability.

Cost discipline and cash generation

Expenses remained well‑controlled, with total G&A at $12.3 million and cash G&A at $8.0 million, or about 5.0% of total revenue, down from 5.9% a year earlier. The company now expects slightly lower cash G&A for the year and generated roughly $40 million of free cash flow after dividends in the quarter, or about $160 million on an annualized basis.

Shareholder returns and modest payout

Investors saw steady income, with the board declaring a quarterly dividend of $0.31 per share. That payment represented a conservative AFFO payout ratio of roughly 62%, leaving meaningful retained cash flow to reinvest in acquisitions and support continued per‑share growth.

Operator-specific bankruptcy and tenant credit event

Not everything was smooth, as one restaurant operator filed for bankruptcy, affecting seven properties and about 30 basis points of annual base rent. Management expects to recover roughly 80% of that exposure through backfills and sales, but the situation required active asset management and highlighted the importance of tenant scrutiny.

Quarterly impairment driven by home furnishings tenant

The company also recorded impairment charges tied primarily to a former home furnishings location previously impacted by a tenant bankruptcy and lease rejection. While the impairment, cited around $16 million, was meaningful at the asset level, management emphasized that it remained isolated within an otherwise stable and diversified portfolio.

Pressure in casual dining & entertainment verticals

Executives flagged ongoing softness in casual dining and entertainment, where sales were generally flat to down 2% to 3% and margins compressed by about 100 to 200 basis points. They estimated this could translate into roughly 10 to 20 basis points of pressure on rent coverage in those specific sub‑sectors, though the overall portfolio impact remains modest.

Potential equity dilution from unsettled forwards

Looking at the capital structure, unsettled forward equity of $541 million at an average price of $30.55 represents a future source of funding but also some dilution risk. Management already builds a conservative $0.01 to $0.02 per‑share impact into full‑year AFFO guidance, and these forward shares are reflected in the diluted share count.

Early 2Q transaction pace uncertainty

The company cautioned that the second quarter started slowly on the deal front and that acquisitions may lag the first quarter’s robust pace. They are modeling 2Q acquisition volume in the $275 million to $325 million range, introducing some timing uncertainty even as the full‑year investment targets move higher.

Moderated disposition volume and idiosyncratic sales timing

On the disposition side, sales activity moderated to $10.2 million in the quarter at a 6.9% cap rate, following a heavier sell‑down late last year. Management reiterated that dispositions are primarily a tool for portfolio shaping and that timing can be lumpy, rather than a consistent driver of accretive capital recycling.

Forward-looking guidance and growth outlook

Guidance for 2026 paints a constructive picture, with AFFO per share expected between $2.00 and $2.05 and acquisition volume guided to $1.1 billion to $1.5 billion, supported by cap rates in the mid‑to‑high 7% range. With low leverage, $1.5 billion of liquidity, modest expected dilution, and about $160 million of annual retained cash flow, management appears confident it can fund growth while maintaining a disciplined cost structure.

Essential Properties Realty Trust’s call framed a company leaning into growth while keeping risk in check, backed by strong earnings, a conservative balance sheet, and high‑quality leases. For investors, the story centers on visible AFFO expansion, attractive acquisition economics, and a prudent payout, with the main watch points being isolated credit issues and the pace of deal execution over the next few quarters.

Fee-based assets in the Private Client Group hit a record $1.04 trillion, up 20% year-over-year, reinforcing the shift toward more recurring revenues. Record Bank Lending and SBL Momentum

Bank loans reached a record $54.8 billion, up 14% from last year and 3% from last quarter, illustrating solid credit demand. Securities-based lending balances were a standout, rising more than $5 billion, or about 31% year-over-year, as clients increasingly tap portfolios to fund liquidity needs.

Capital Deployment and Balance Sheet Strength

The firm continued to aggressively return capital, repurchasing $400 million of stock in the quarter at an average $155 per share and $1.6 billion over the past year. Including dividends, more than $2 billion—roughly 94% of earnings—was returned to common shareholders, while capital ratios remained strong with Tier 1 leverage at 12.4% and total capital at 24%.

Recruiting and Adviser Retention Tailwinds

Adviser recruiting remained a key growth engine, with trailing 12-month recruited production at $141 million tied to nearly $21 billion in client assets at prior firms. Management framed this as the second-strongest quarter historically for recruited production and assets, supported by a robust pipeline and high adviser retention across the platform.

Capital Markets and Investment Banking Rebound

Capital Markets net revenues rose to $464 million with pretax income of $51 million, improving both year-over-year and sequentially as underwriting and M&A activity picked up. March was described as particularly strong, and a healthy pipeline plus strategic deals like GreensLedge and the pending Clark Capital acquisition suggest further momentum if markets cooperate.

Technology and AI Spend Begins to Pay Off

Annual technology spending now exceeds $1.1 billion, underscoring Raymond James’s push to modernize its platform. Management highlighted early success with a proprietary AI operations agent rolled out to several hundred advisers and home-office teams, citing strong initial feedback and a goal of boosting adviser efficiency and client experience.

Interest-Related Revenue and NII Under Pressure

The softer side of the quarter showed up in interest-sensitive lines, as combined net interest income and RJBDP fees slipped 3% sequentially to $650 million. The average yield on RJBDP balances fell 6 basis points to 2.7% after recent Fed cuts, trimming interest-related revenues even as other businesses offset the damage.

PCG Profitability Hit by Lower Rates

Despite record revenues, the Private Client Group’s pretax income came in at $416 million, down 3% year-over-year. Management pointed to lower short-term rates reducing non-interest revenues, reminding investors that PCG earnings remain partly exposed to the rate backdrop even amid strong asset growth.

Higher Compensation and Payout Pressures

Compensation expense climbed to $2.54 billion, driving the total compensation ratio to 65.8% and limiting operating leverage. The firm cited recruiting costs and a shift toward higher-payout independent advisers as key drivers, reflecting a strategic trade-off between near-term margins and long-term growth in adviser productivity and assets.

Non-Compensation Costs Move Higher

Non-compensation expenses rose 10% year-over-year and 5% sequentially to $583 million, largely tied to ongoing investments across the platform. While management framed this spending as necessary to support growth and technology initiatives, the pace of cost growth is a factor investors will track closely against future revenue gains.

Cash Sweep Balances and Liquidity Trends

Domestic cash sweep and enhanced savings balances ended at $57.8 billion, down 1% sequentially and representing 3.7% of domestic PCG assets. Management pointed to seasonal April pressure from record fee billings of about $1.9 billion and tax outflows, while also noting an industry-wide drift toward lower transactional cash per account.

Tax Rate Volatility and Outlook

The quarterly effective tax rate rose to 26%, above the firm’s longer-term target, due to nondeductible losses on corporate-owned life insurance. Management reiterated its expectation for a lower 2026 effective tax rate in the mid-20% range, suggesting the latest quarter’s bump should not be extrapolated.

Pipeline Conversion and Capital Ratio Trends

Management described the investment banking pipeline as strong but warned that deal conversion timing remains uncertain amid geopolitical risks and sector-specific concerns, particularly in technology and software. Meanwhile, the Tier 1 leverage ratio declined about 90 basis points over the past year due to balance sheet growth and capital returns, though leadership still views current levels as conservative.

Guidance and Forward Outlook

Looking ahead to fiscal Q3 2026, Raymond James expects asset management and related fees to rise about 1% versus Q2, helped by an extra billing day and record fee-based assets. Assuming stable rates and balances, combined net interest income and RJBDP fees are also projected to edge up about 1%, while management reaffirmed a mid-20% effective tax rate target and highlighted ample excess capital and liquidity to support continued buybacks and strategic growth.

Raymond James’s earnings call painted a picture of a firm leaning into growth while navigating rate and cost pressures with confidence. Record revenues, fee-based asset gains, strong lending, and aggressive capital returns all point to durable momentum, even as investors watch interest income, expenses, and capital markets conversion risk as the next chapters unfold.

Total underwriting and other expenses were $22.3 million, producing an expense ratio of 29.7%, slightly better than 29.9% a year earlier. This marked the third straight year-over-year improvement, driven by operating leverage as premium volume grows faster than overhead.

Prior-Year Reserve Releases Aid Loss Ratio

Prior accident years produced $7.6 million of favorable development, equal to 10.1 points of benefit in the quarter. This reserve release contributed to a net loss ratio of 61.9%, cushioning the impact of current accident-year loss pressures.

Payroll and Wage Trends Support Premiums

Targeted industry classes showed healthy underlying payroll trends, with wage growth of 4.5% in the quarter while headcount stayed essentially flat. That mix of higher wages on stable employee counts helped lift premium levels even as rate pressure persisted.

Improving Investment Yield, High-Quality Portfolio

On the investment side, the tax-equivalent portfolio yield rose to 3.9%, up 7 basis points year over year, with new investments yielding 174 basis points more than assets rolling off. The portfolio maintained an average AA- credit rating and a 4.4-year duration, with asset allocation largely unchanged to preserve quality.

Capital Strength and Shareholder Returns

AMERISAFE closed the quarter with roughly $774 million in cash and invested assets, underscoring a solid capital position. The company repurchased nearly 120,000 shares for $4.0 million at an average price of $33.60, leaving $12.9 million authorized and signaling confidence in long-term value.

Lower Large Claims Temper Volatility

The quarter saw no claims with incurred value above $1 million, compared with two such large losses a year earlier. This absence of outsized claims reduced earnings volatility and supported the stability of the reported loss ratio.

Competitive Pricing Pressures Weigh on Rates

Management stressed that the workers’ compensation landscape remains highly competitive, with prolonged soft pricing across the industry. Downward pressure from filed loss costs is creating mild upward pressure on current accident-year loss ratios as insurers compete on rate.

Current Accident-Year Loss Ratio Drifts Higher

The current accident-year loss ratio came in at 72% for the quarter, matching the 12-month figure from last year but above the 71% level in Q1 2025. This modest uptick highlights the tension between rate compression and underlying claim severity trends.

Earnings Decline Despite Operating Discipline

Operating net income fell to $9.5 million, or $0.50 per diluted share, from $11.4 million, or $0.60, in Q1 2025. GAAP net income also eased to $8.1 million, or $0.43 per share, from $8.9 million, reflecting softer reserve releases and investment income.

Audit premium and related adjustments totaled $3.7 million in the quarter, down from $5.0 million in the prior-year period. The reduced contribution from audits partially offset underlying premium growth, trimming the top-line benefit from exposure reviews.

Smaller Reserve Releases Versus Last Year

Favorable prior-year development declined to $7.6 million, or 10.1 points, from $8.7 million, or 12.7 points, in Q1 2025. While still positive, the smaller reserve release reduced one source of earnings lift compared with the prior-year quarter.

Net Investment Income Slightly Softer

Net investment income slipped 0.8% to $6.6 million, pressured by lower average investable assets. This came despite better new-money yields, showing that balance sheet dynamics can offset rate-driven income gains.

Unrealized Losses from Higher Rates

Held-to-maturity securities carried about $7.9 million of net unrealized losses at quarter-end, driven by interest rate movements. These mark-to-market pressures do not flow through book value but highlight ongoing rate sensitivity in the bond portfolio.
